name: Arnhem
classification_notes: Arnhem is a text-optimised serif designed for demanding newspaper conditions, with strong colour, moderate contrast and sturdy construction typical of a transitional model adapted for continuous reading.
summary: A Dutch text serif by Fred Smeijers, originally cut for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, built for strong colour at small sizes and difficult print conditions.
description: Designed by Fred Smeijers for the daily newspaper of the Dutch state, Arnhem prioritises legibility under adverse printing conditions with a deliberately sturdy colour throughout its weight range. The family runs from Blond to Black, each with roman and italic, plus small caps, lining figures, oldstyle figures and tabular figures as OpenType features. A titling variant (Arnhem Fine) and headline variant (Arnhem Display) extend the system for larger-size work beyond the core text styles.
foundry_blurb: Arnhem was initially designed by Fred Smeijers for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, the daily newspaper of the Dutch state. The foundry describes it as a highly functional design for long text, offered in four weights each with roman and italic, small caps and multiple figure sets. Arnhem Normal carries strong colour suited to small sizes and suboptimal printing; the Blond weight sits lighter than Normal but heavier than a conventional Light, for settings where that stronger colour is unwanted.

name: Arnhem Greek
classification_notes: Arnhem is described as a functional text serif originally designed for a Dutch state newspaper; its even colour, moderate contrast and sturdy construction place it in the transitional serif tradition adapted for demanding print conditions.
summary: Fred Smeijers's Arnhem extended to Greek script in 2020, giving the five-weight text family full coverage across Latin and Greek for long-form setting.
description: Originally drawn for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, Arnhem is built for demanding text conditions: strong colour at small sizes, sturdy construction and a notably deliberate weight range that replaces a conventional Light with a heavier Blond. The Greek expansion adds ten styles covering all five weights in roman and italic, with small capitals, lining and oldstyle figures, and a full suite of OpenType features. The family sits alongside the Latin-only Arnhem Fine and Arnhem Display subfamilies for titling and headline work respectively.
foundry_blurb: Arnhem was designed by Fred Smeijers for the Nederlandse Staatscourant and is classified as a functional text typeface suited to long-form setting. The family runs from Blond to Black, each with roman and italic, and in 2020 was extended to cover Greek and Cyrillic scripts. Arnhem Fine and Arnhem Display accompany the text cuts for titling and newspaper headline use, both available in Latin only.

name: Arnhem Cyrillic
classification_notes: Arnhem is described as a functional text serif originally designed for a Dutch state newspaper; its robust stroke contrast, small caps and range of weights place it firmly in the transitional serif tradition.
summary: Fred Smeijers's newspaper text serif Arnhem extended to cover Cyrillic, offered in five weights each with matching italics and small caps.
description: Originally drawn for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, Arnhem is a text serif built for demanding conditions: small sizes, suboptimal print, high colour. The Cyrillic extension adds full coverage for Russian and a broad range of related scripts across all ten styles, retaining the small caps, lining and oldstyle figures, and the distinctive Blond weight that sits heavier than a conventional Light. OpenType features include case-sensitive forms, fractions, superscripts and scientific inferiors.
foundry_blurb: Arnhem, by Fred Smeijers, was originally designed for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, the daily newspaper of the Dutch state. Classified as a highly functional text design, the family runs across five weights from Blond to Black, each with roman and italic cuts and matching small caps, lining figures, non-lining figures and small cap figures. The Cyrillic edition was released in 2020.

name: Arnhem Fine
classification_notes: Arnhem Fine is a formal serif with crisp display-oriented details including sharp serifs and refined curve connections; it is the titling companion to the Arnhem text family, featuring longer ascenders and descenders and a smaller x-height suited to headline and title setting.
summary: A titling companion to the Arnhem text family, drawn with sharper serifs, longer extenders and a reduced x-height for use at headline and title sizes.
description: Arnhem Fine sits at the display end of the Arnhem superfamily, sharing the formal character of the text cuts but redrawn with crisper details and proportions suited to large sizes. The longer ascenders and descenders give it an elegant page presence, while the smaller x-height and refined curve connections make the drawing more apparent at titling scales. Four weights from Normal to Bold each carry a matching italic, alongside small caps, oldstyle figures and an extensive OpenType feature set.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Arnhem Fine as the titling companion to the Arnhem family, featuring crisper display details such as sharper serifs and refined curve connections, along with longer ascenders and descenders and a smaller x-height than the text fonts. It complements both Arnhem and Arnhem Display, completing the Arnhem superfamily, and includes small caps and non-lining figures across an efficient weight range of romans and matching italics.

name: Arnhem Display
classification_notes: Arnhem Display is a transitional serif with roots in traditional typography, originally developed for newspaper headline composition with crisp, high-contrast letterforms suited to large-size editorial use.
summary: A display-optimised serif by Fred Smeijers, developed for Het Financieele Dagblad and rooted in the Arnhem family originally drawn for the Dutch government newspaper.
description: Designed in 2006 for the redesign of Het Financieele Dagblad, this display cut of Arnhem sharpens the original text family's details for headline use, with gracefully tailored italics and crisp contrast across three weights. The original Arnhem was drawn by Fred Smeijers in 1999 for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, giving the family a grounding in serious Dutch editorial practice. OpenType features include oldstyle and lining figures in both tabular and proportional widths, fractions, case-sensitive punctuation and a full range of accented characters for Western, Central and Eastern European Latin languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Arnhem Display as a member of the Arnhem typeface family designed for newspaper headline composition. The original Arnhem was designed by Fred Smeijers in 1999 for the Nederlandse Staatscourant, and Arnhem Display was first created in 2006 for Mark Porter's redesign of Het Financieele Dagblad. Erik Spiekermann is quoted describing Arnhem as having 'an edge', and TYPE BY note that its character, colour and reading quality make it exceptional as an editorial design tool.

name: Bery Roman
classification_notes: Bery Roman is a stencil typeface reviving 18th-century French stencil letters, with calligraphic, engraved and typographic qualities that place it firmly in the display category despite its versatility for text settings.
summary: A revival of 18th-century French stencil letters by Fred Smeijers, drawn from the stencil set Jean Gabriel Bery supplied to Benjamin Franklin in 1781.
description: Designed by Fred Smeijers and part of TYPE BY's Stencil Fonts Series, it reconstructs the roman letters of Paris stencil maker Jean Gabriel Bery, whose work blends calligraphic, engraved and typographic sensibilities characteristic of the French stencil tradition. Smeijers recognised the purely typographic potential in Bery's originals, producing a single style that works at both text and display sizes. True to the historical source, not every glyph carries a stencil break, reflecting the period view that breaks should be as few and inconspicuous as possible.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describes Bery Roman as based on the stencil letters of Jean Gabriel Bery, a Paris stencil maker whose work is best known from the stencil set he supplied to Benjamin Franklin in 1781, now held at the American Philosophical Society in Philadelphia. Fred Smeijers identified the typographic potential in Bery's letters and designed the revival to bring those qualities forward, resulting in a design suited to both text and display use. The typeface launched as part of the Stencil Fonts Series on 19 April 2012 to accompany the exhibition 'Between Writing & Type: the Stencil Letter' in Antwerp.

name: Bery Script
classification_notes: A script typeface derived from 18th-century French stencil letters in the batârde coulée calligraphic tradition, with stencil breaks built into the design as a structural feature rather than a limitation.
summary: A single-weight stencil script by Fred Smeijers, drawn from the 18th-century French stencil letters of Paris stencil maker Jean Gabriel Bery.
description: Fred Smeijers adapted the batârde coulée calligraphic hand of Jean Gabriel Bery, whose stencil set was supplied to Benjamin Franklin in 1781 and is now held at the American Philosophical Society in Philadelphia. The stencil breaks, rather than undermining the script's rhythm, introduce a syncopation that works across text settings, aided by lowercase linking strokes added by Smeijers. Released in 2012 as part of TYPE BY's Stencil Fonts Series, it is paired with Bery Roman and sits within a body of work that accompanied an exhibition on the history of the stencil letter curated by Smeijers and Eric Kindel.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Bery Script as part of their Stencil Fonts Series, designed by Fred Smeijers and based on the stencil letters of Jean Gabriel Bery, a Paris stencil maker whose work was supplied to Benjamin Franklin in 1781. They note that translating the batârde coulée into stencils, and then into type, might seem to work against the hand's inherent qualities, but argue that Bery and Smeijers have succeeded in making the stencil breaks a source of happy syncopation. The glyph set is substantially extended from Bery's originals, including new lowercase linking strokes that add rhythmic counterpoint in text settings.

name: Bery Tuscan
classification_notes: A historical revival of Jean Gabriel Bery's 18th-century tuscan stencil letters, characterised by bifurcated serif-tendrils and acanthus-leaf motifs; firmly display in category due to its ornamental, decorative letterforms unsuited to continuous text.
summary: A digital revival of Jean Gabriel Bery's 18th-century Parisian tuscan stencil letters, drawn by Fred Smeijers and Pierre Pané-Farré from just sixteen surviving stencils.
description: Based on the capital stencil set Bery supplied to Benjamin Franklin in 1781, the design features acanthus-leaf strokes ending in rounded, bifurcated serif-tendrils. Smeijers and Pané-Farré extrapolated a complete uppercase alphabet from only sixteen original stencils, translating the slanted originals into an upright configuration and producing what is claimed to be the largest glyph set ever assembled for a tuscan typeface. Released in 2012 as part of the Stencil Fonts Series, it sits alongside Bery Roman and Bery Script as historical revivals rooted in a long French stencil tradition.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Bery Tuscan as a revival of the tuscan stencil letters made by Paris stencil maker Jean Gabriel Bery, whose work is known chiefly from the set he supplied to Benjamin Franklin in 1781, now held at the American Philosophical Society Museum in Philadelphia. Designed by Fred Smeijers with assistance from Pierre Pané-Farré, the typeface was extrapolated from sixteen surviving stencils, with the slanted originals redrawn in an upright configuration and a full complement of new glyphs invented to complete the alphabet. It was released in 2012 as part of the Stencil Fonts Series, launched to coincide with the exhibition 'Between Writing & Type: the Stencil Letter' at Catapult Gallery, Antwerp.

name: Blatt
classification_notes: Blatt presents as a contemporary grotesque with a broad weight range from Thin to Black, featuring upright and italic cuts and a named weight system typical of European grotesque traditions.
summary: A 16-style grotesque from TYPE BY spanning eight weights from Thin to Black, each with a matching italic.
description: Sixteen styles run across eight named weights, from Thin and Blond through to Bold and Black, each paired with an italic. The OpenType feature set is thorough, covering small capitals, oldstyle and lining figures, proportional and tabular variants, fractions, scientific inferiors and a stylistic set. Latin Extended-A and -B coverage makes it suitable for Central and Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Blatt as a 16-style grotesque family covering eight weights from Thin to Black with matching italics, and a full suite of OpenType features including small capitals, multiple figure styles, fractions and case-sensitive forms.

name: Custodia
classification_notes: Custodia draws on seventeenth-century letterform conventions with an upright, formal structure typical of Dutch Baroque typefaces, placing it firmly in the historical serif tradition rather than transitional or old-style categories.
summary: A seventeenth-century-flavoured serif designed by Fred Smeijers in 2002 for the Custodia Foundation in Paris, commercially released in 2003.
description: Originally commissioned for the publications of the Custodia Foundation in Paris, this typeface carries the upright formality and refined detail of Dutch Baroque letterforms. The family offers three weights with matching italics, and the character set is extensive: small capitals, lining and oldstyle figures, small-cap-height figures, discretionary ligatures, fractions, superiors and inferiors. Coverage extends across Latin Extended 1, supporting a broad range of European languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Custodia as an elegant and refined typeface with a seventeenth-century flavour, designed by Fred Smeijers in 2002 for the Custodia Foundation in Paris and commercially released in 2003. The fonts include small caps, multiple figure sets, discretionary ligatures, fractions, superiors and inferiors, and support the Latin Extended 1 character set for multilingual Latin-script composition.

name: Fresco
classification_notes: Fresco is a contemporary humanist serif with roots in traditional type-making, designed by Fred Smeijers whose work draws on historical punchcutting practice. Its text-first design and warm, readable character place it firmly in the humanist serif tradition.
summary: A large multipurpose humanist serif by Fred Smeijers, first shown in 1998 and built for serious text setting across editorial, corporate and book contexts.
description: Designed by Fred Smeijers and commercially released in 2003, Fresco draws on the rhythms of traditional punchcutting while remaining a distinctly contemporary text face. The superfamily spans six sub-families including Fresco Plus, which offers slightly extended ascenders and descenders for closer typographic control, along with sans, informal and script variants. Both Fresco and Fresco Plus share identical kerning and spacing, allowing them to be mixed within a single body of text without disruption.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Fresco as a large multipurpose type family designed by Fred Smeijers, first shown in the Dutch design magazine Items in October 1998 and commercially released in 2003. They characterise it as a refreshment of traditional and conventional issues — a contemporary typeface that embraces the strengths of typographic tradition — suitable for serious reading matter, corporate use and magazines. The Fresco superfamily includes Fresco, Fresco Sans, Fresco Plus, Fresco Plus Sans, Fresco Informal and Fresco Script, with the Fresco Plus variation released in March 2006 offering slightly longer ascenders and descenders for particular typographic use.

name: Fresco Sans
classification_notes: Fresco Sans exhibits humanist sans-serif characteristics with a warm, readable structure across its three sub-families. The range of weights, italics and condensed variants, combined with extensive OpenType features including small caps and oldstyle figures, is consistent with a text-oriented humanist approach.
summary: A 30-style humanist sans superfamily spanning three sub-families, each in five weights with italics, and a condensed variant for tighter settings.
description: The family is divided into Fresco Sans, Fresco Sans Plus and Fresco Sans Condensed, each running from Light to Black with matching italics, giving 30 styles in total. OpenType features are well provisioned across most cuts, including small capitals, oldstyle figures, fractions, superscripts and scientific inferiors. The condensed sub-family covers the same weight range with a reduced feature set, making it practical for space-constrained editorial and publication work.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Fresco Sans as a 30-style super family comprising three distinct sub-families: Fresco Sans, Fresco Sans Plus and Fresco Sans Condensed, each available in five weights from Light to Black with corresponding italics and a broad set of OpenType features.

name: Fresco Informal
classification_notes: Fresco Informal is a humanist serif with a relaxed, informal character that sits comfortably between text and display use. Its warmth and organic quality align it with the humanist serif tradition rather than transitional or old-style models.
summary: The informal branch of the Fresco superfamily, offering five weights with italics and a character suited to everyday editorial and communications work.
description: Part of a wider superfamily that includes Fresco, Fresco Sans, Fresco Plus, Fresco Plus Sans and Fresco Script, the Informal variant shares the same x-height and capital proportions as its siblings, allowing mixing across the family. Its relaxed character makes it less suited to formal documents but handles well at small text sizes, and becomes particularly expressive at display scale. Ten styles span Light to Black with matching italics throughout, and both lining and oldstyle figures are included.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Fresco Informal as the more playful part of the Fresco family, noting that its design proportions match the text fonts so styles can be freely combined. Despite its name, they say it performs well at small sizes, though its character is less suited to formal texts; at larger sizes it is described as handsome and well suited to contemporary work aimed at broad audiences.

name: Fresco Script
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a script typeface and the least formal part of the Fresco superfamily, with strokes rooted in a loose interpretation of classical humanist italic rather than calligraphic tradition.
summary: A loose, semi-formal script rooted in humanist italic, designed without connecting strokes and available in four weights.
description: Part of the broader Fresco superfamily, this script favours the rhythm of quick, contemporary handwriting over calligraphic formality. Its structure draws from humanist italic but strips away the decorative connectors found in many older script faces, keeping the forms open and readable. Four weights from Light to Black give it some range for display and short-copy use.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Fresco Script as the least formal member of the Fresco superfamily, with strokes that are loose and spontaneous yet controlled enough to remain legible. The backbone of each letter follows a loose interpretation of the classical humanist italic, giving the typeface the feeling of quick semi-formal handwriting made with a contemporary tool, free from calligraphic atmosphere or fantasy flourishes.

name: Haultin
classification_notes: The range of weights, italic companions and naming conventions suggest a humanist sans with broad text utility; the specimen copy referencing sixteenth-century punchcutting reinforces a connection to historical type culture, consistent with humanist sans sensibilities.
summary: A 24-style humanist sans from TYPE BY spanning twelve weights, each with a matching italic and a full set of OpenType features for text work.
description: Twelve weights run from Thin to Black, with each accompanied by an italic, giving 24 styles in total. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions, superscript, scientific inferiors and a slashed zero, making it well-equipped for demanding editorial and typographic work. The naming of intermediate weights as Blond, NormalLight and NormalDark adds fine-grained control in the middle of the range where most text setting sits.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Haultin as a 24-style family with a weight range from Thin to Black, each style paired with an italic. The family ships with extensive OpenType support covering small capitals, multiple figure styles, fractions and stylistic alternates.

name: Ludwig
classification_notes: Ludwig presents as a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a wide range of weights and widths. Its structure, weight range from Thin to ExtraBlack, and condensed variants are consistent with the grotesque tradition.
summary: A 48-style grotesque superfamily spanning three widths and eight weights, from Thin to ExtraBlack, each with matching italics.
description: Ludwig is offered in three width variants — standard, SemiCondensed and Condensed — each running across eight weights from Thin to ExtraBlack with accompanying italics, totalling 48 styles. The family includes a broad set of OpenType features including small capitals, old-style figures, tabular figures, fractions and multiple stylistic sets. Its range of widths and weights makes it suited to editorial and identity work requiring consistent type across varied column measures and hierarchies.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Ludwig as a super family of 48 styles, organised across three width variants — Ludwig, Ludwig SemiCondensed and Ludwig Condensed — each available in eight weights from Thin to ExtraBlack with italic counterparts throughout.

name: Ludwig Outline
classification_notes: Ludwig Outline is a display variant of the Ludwig grotesque family, rendered as an outline form intended for headline and decorative use rather than text setting. Its grotesque foundations are rooted in early 19th-century German sanserif models.
summary: An outline display variant of Fred Smeijers' Ludwig grotesque, drawn from early 19th-century German sanserif models and released as a single Normal style in 2019.
description: Derived from the Ludwig text family, this outline cut strips the grotesque back to its contour, making it suited to packaging, posters and headline work where open letterforms can breathe. Ludwig itself draws on the earliest German grotesques rather than later rationalist revivals, giving it a slight irregularity that distinguishes it from smoother neo-grotesque alternatives. The outline version was designed alongside Ludwig Shaded between 2007 and 2009 but held back until a fresh release in 2019.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Ludwig Outline as a display typeface by Fred Smeijers, one of his latest releases. The broader Ludwig family was commercially released in 2009, rooted in the first German sanserifs suitable for text, though Smeijers frames it not as a revival but as a personal interpretation of how an early 19th-century sanserif might look and perform today. The outline and shaded variants were designed in 2007-2009 and freshly released in 2019.

name: Ludwig Shaded
classification_notes: A decorative shaded variant of Ludwig, itself a grotesque sans rooted in early 19th-century German sanserif tradition. The shaded treatment places it firmly in the display category despite the base family's text capabilities.
summary: A shaded display cut of Fred Smeijers' Ludwig grotesque, originally drawn in 2007-2009 and released commercially in 2019.
description: Ludwig Shaded is one of two decorative variants — alongside Ludwig Outline — drawn from Smeijers' Ludwig family, a grotesque rooted in early German sanserifs of the mid-19th century. The single Normal weight carries the same considered letterforms as the text family, with their characteristic period clumsiness, now amplified for headline and poster work through the shaded treatment. It shares proportions with TYPE BY's Arnhem, making it a natural companion for mixed-family settings.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Ludwig Shaded as a display typeface designed by Fred Smeijers, originally created in 2007-2009 alongside Ludwig Outline and freshly released in 2019. The broader Ludwig family is grounded in early German grotesques and designed as a companion to Arnhem, sharing x-height, cap-height and descender proportions across both families.

name: Mersey
classification_notes: The page presents a large sans-serif superfamily with weight names typical of grotesque traditions; the multiple stylistic sets and sub-families suggest a systematic grotesque with optical or stylistic variations across seven numbered sub-families.
summary: A 49-style grotesque superfamily from TYPE BY, built across seven numbered sub-families each offering seven weights from Thin to Black.
description: Each of the seven sub-families shares the same weight range -- Thin, Light, Blond, Normal, SemiBold, Bold and Black -- giving 49 styles in total across the superfamily. Up to 18 stylistic sets per style suggest considerable alternate glyph coverage for typographic flexibility. The numbered sub-family structure points to a systematic approach where stylistic differences between variants allow considered mixing within a single project.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Mersey as a superfamily of 49 styles organised into seven sub-families, each running seven weights from Thin to Black, with extensive OpenType feature support including fractions, ordinals, slashed zero and up to 18 stylistic sets.

name: Monitor
classification_notes: Monitor presents as a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a wide weight range and multiple width variants, consistent with the grotesque tradition of workhorse extended families built for demanding typographic systems.
summary: A 54-style grotesque superfamily from TYPE BY spanning three widths and nine weights, each with matching italics.
description: Spread across standard, SemiCondensed and Condensed widths, Monitor offers nine weights from Thin to ExtraBlack with italic companions throughout, giving designers 54 styles in total. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions and a slashed zero, making it a practical choice for complex typographic systems. The range of widths and the density of the weight scale suit editorial, branding and display work where flexibility across a single family is required.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Monitor as a super family of 54 styles, available as the full collection or as individual sub-families: Monitor, Monitor SemiCondensed and Monitor Condensed, each comprising 18 styles across nine weights with italics.

name: Puncho
classification_notes: Puncho is a stencil display typeface derived from metal stencil letter punches, with letterforms shaped by the mechanical constraints of small-scale punch manufacture rather than conventional type design conventions.
summary: A stencil typeface by Fred Smeijers based on 19th-century metal letter punches made by S. M. Spencer of Boston, with letterforms constrained by the mechanical realities of their original manufacture at 1/8 inch.
description: Originally released as part of the OurType Stencil Series in 2012, Puncho debuted alongside an exhibition on stencil letter history curated by Eric Kindel and Smeijers at Catapult gallery in Antwerp. Its unusual letterforms trace directly to Spencer's tiny punch-made stencils, where minimal size and industrial process dictated simple, sturdy shapes. Available in a single weight covering an extended Latin character set.
foundry_blurb: Puncho is designed by Fred Smeijers and based on stencil letter punches made by S. M. Spencer of Boston, with the original letters measuring just 1/8 inch. The mechanical constraints of that manufacture are described as the source of Puncho's distinctive shapes. It was first released in 2012 to coincide with the exhibition 'Between Writing & Type: the Stencil Letter' in Antwerp, and is one in a series of stencil fonts from the OurType Stencil Series.

name: Quadraat
classification_notes: Quadraat is a serif rooted in Renaissance letterform traditions, drawing on humanist proportions while incorporating contemporary ideas on construction. Its origins in the FF Quadraat family place it firmly in the humanist old-style camp.
summary: Fred Smeijers's debut commercial typeface, first released through FontFont in 1992, marrying Renaissance proportions with a quietly assertive contemporary construction.
description: Originally drawn for a Dutch design company of the same name of which Smeijers was a co-founder, Quadraat balances Renaissance elegance against a deliberate restraint that keeps it from feeling either decorative or dated. The TYPE BY edition spans ten styles across five weights from Light to Black, each with a matching italic, and carries an extensive OpenType feature set including small capitals, multiple figure styles, historical forms and stylistic alternates for K and R. The family sits equally well in long-form book typography and considered editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Quadraat as Fred Smeijers's first commercially released typeface, originally issued by FontFont in 1992 and designed for the Dutch design company het Lab of which Smeijers was a co-founder. They note that the family combines Renaissance elegance with contemporary ideas on construction and form, and that it aims for an understated noticeability rarely found in typefaces of its era.

name: Quadraat Sans
classification_notes: Quadraat Sans is the sans-serif companion to the Quadraat serif family, exhibiting humanist proportions and the calligraphic warmth typical of humanist sans designs rooted in Renaissance letterform traditions.
summary: A 28-style humanist sans superfamily spanning regular and condensed widths across seven weights, with extensive OpenType features and Cyrillic support.
description: The sans-serif companion to the Quadraat family, this superfamily runs across 14 regular styles and 14 condensed styles, each with matching italics from Thin to Black. Humanist proportions give it warmth at text sizes, while the condensed subfamily extends its range into tighter measures and display work. OpenType features include small capitals, multiple figure sets, fractions, historical forms and a slashed zero, with both Latin and Cyrillic scripts covered.
foundry_blurb: Quadraat Sans is presented as a super family of 28 styles, divided equally between the standard and condensed widths, each running from Thin through to Black with corresponding italics. The family supports a broad set of OpenType features including small capitals, tabular and proportional figures, discretionary ligatures, fractions, superscript, subscript, case-sensitive forms, historical forms and a slashed zero.

name: Quadraat Sans Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of the Quadraat Sans family, which draws from Renaissance letterform traditions and humanist construction; the fixed-width format places it firmly in the mono category.
summary: A monospaced variant of Fred Smeijers' Quadraat Sans, remastered in 2019, bringing humanist warmth to a genre that typically favours the clinical.
description: Quadraat Sans Mono carries the characterful, Renaissance-inflected outlines of the broader Quadraat superfamily into a fixed-width format. The family comes in Regular and Bold, each with a matching italic, and supports an extensive range of Western, Central and Eastern European Latin languages. Remastered and re-hinted in 2019, it suits coding environments and editorial contexts where a monospaced face with personality is preferred over a neutral default.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Quadraat Sans Mono as a monospaced variant of the Quadraat superfamily that brings an unruly, characterful energy to a genre typically defined by shyness and calculation. Quadraat itself is Fred Smeijers' first commercially released typeface, originally released through FontFont in 1992 and combining Renaissance elegance with contemporary construction; the Sans followed in 1996. The fonts were fully remastered in 2019 and re-hinted for current digital technology, and the character set covers figures, fractions, mathematical and monetary symbols, ligatures and accents for all Western, Central and Eastern European Latin-based languages.

name: Sansa
classification_notes: Sansa presents as a humanist sans-serif with a range of weights and widths typical of the genre; the family structure with Light through UltraBlack across standard, semi-condensed and condensed widths is consistent with contemporary text-and-display humanist sans families.
summary: A 32-style superfamily spanning three widths and up to six weights, with full italic coverage and a broad OpenType feature set.
description: Sansa is structured across three subfamilies — standard, SemiCondensed and Condensed — each offering Light through Black weights with matching italics, and the Condensed cut extending to UltraBlack for headline use. The OpenType feature set is thorough, covering small capitals, oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions, stylistic alternates and more, making the family well-suited to both text and display work. The 32-style range gives it the coverage expected of a workhorse family for editorial or identity systems.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Sansa as a superfamily of 32 styles, comprising three width variants — Sansa, Sansa SemiCondensed and Sansa Condensed — each with multiple weights and italics, and an extensive set of OpenType features including small capitals, figure variants, fractions and stylistic alternates.

name: Sansa Greek
classification_notes: Sansa Greek is a sans-serif family with humanist proportions, presented across regular, semi-condensed and condensed widths with a full Greek script character set.
summary: A 30-style Greek sans-serif superfamily spanning regular, semi-condensed and condensed widths across five weights with matching italics.
description: The family covers three width variants — standard, semi-condensed and condensed — each running from Light to Black with italic companions, giving 30 styles in total. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions, case-sensitive forms, stylistic alternates and a slashed zero. Suited to Greek-language editorial and identity work where width flexibility and weight range matter.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Sansa Greek as a superfamily of 30 styles, with the full range divided into Sansa GRK, Sansa SmCon GRK and Sansa Con GRK subfamilies, each offering Light through Black weights with italics and an extensive set of OpenType features including small capitals, multiple numeral styles and stylistic alternates.

name: Sansa Cyrillic
classification_notes: Sansa Cyrillic is a sans-serif with humanist proportions extended to Cyrillic script, offered across regular, semi-condensed and condensed width variants with a full italic range.
summary: A 30-style Cyrillic sans-serif family spanning three widths from regular to condensed, each in five weights with matching italics.
description: The family is structured across three sub-families — Sansa CYR, Sansa SmCon CYR and Sansa Con CYR — giving ten styles per width for a total of 30. Each sub-family runs from Light to Black with paired italics throughout. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions, case-sensitive forms and stylistic alternates, making it well-equipped for demanding editorial and UI work in Cyrillic and Latin.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Sansa Cyrillic as a super family of 30 styles built across three width variants — regular, semi-condensed and condensed — each carrying five weights with italics. The family supports both Cyrillic and Latin scripts and includes an extensive OpenType feature set covering small capitals, multiple numeral styles, fractions, historical forms and stylistic alternates.

name: Sansa Arabic
classification_notes: Sansa is a sanserif superfamily by Fred Smeijers with humanist proportions and a warm, characterful voice; the Arabic extension follows the same design principles across its four weights.
summary: The Arabic extension of Fred Smeijers's Sansa superfamily, released in 2020 across four weights with full Latin support alongside Arabic script.
description: Part of the broader Sansa superfamily originally designed by Fred Smeijers for his own correspondence and released as a retail design in 2002, the Arabic variant arrived in 2020 alongside Greek and Cyrillic additions. Four weights run from Light to Bold, covering Arabic, Persian and Urdu script forms alongside an extensive Latin character set with small caps, multiple figure styles and comprehensive OpenType features. The parent family spans three widths and five weights with matching italics in its Latin form, giving designers a consistent typographic system across scripts.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Sansa as a sanserif superfamily designed by Fred Smeijers that benefits from the liberties of recent sanserif design without overdoing them, calling it a design full of character proven versatile across packaging, posters, book typography and forms. Originally made for Smeijers's own correspondence, it was released as a retail family in 2002 and updated through 2006, 2012 and 2020, when Arabic, Greek and Cyrillic support was added. The Sansa Arabic family offers four weights and supports the full Latin script alongside Arabic.

name: Sansa Slab
classification_notes: A slab serif superfamily with structured, upright letterforms and consistent stroke weights typical of geometric slab designs, offered across three width variants and a broad weight range.
summary: A 32-style slab serif superfamily spanning three widths and six weights, with UltraBlack reserved for the Condensed cut.
description: The family runs across three widths — standard, SmCondensed and Condensed — each with a full italic complement and OpenType features including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures and fractions. The Condensed subfamily extends furthest in weight, reaching UltraBlack, making it the go-to cut for high-impact display settings. Comprehensive figure and case-sensitive options give it practical range across editorial, publication and identity work.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY presents Sansa Slab as a 32-style superfamily comprising standard, SmCondensed and Condensed widths, each available in multiple weights with italics and a full suite of OpenType features including small caps, multiple figure styles, fractions, superscripts and slashed zero.

name: Sansa Soft
classification_notes: Sansa Soft is a humanist sans-serif superfamily with softened terminals and a warm, approachable character, spanning regular, semi-condensed and condensed widths across multiple weights.
summary: A 32-style humanist sans superfamily with softened terminals, spanning three widths from regular through semi-condensed to condensed.
description: The family is organised into three width subfamilies — Sansa Soft, Sansa Soft SemiCondensed and Sansa Soft Condensed — each with italic companions and a weight range running from Light to Black, with an UltraBlack reserved for the Condensed. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions and a slashed zero, giving the family solid utility for both text and display settings. The softened stroke endings lend warmth without tipping into overtly rounded territory.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Sansa Soft as a 32-style superfamily available across three width variants — Sansa Soft, Sansa Soft SemiCondensed and Sansa Soft Condensed — with each subfamily offering a full range of weights and italics, supported by an extensive set of OpenType features.

name: Aktuell Display
classification_notes: A display-optimised serif with broad-nib contrast, placing it firmly in the high-contrast display serif category. It extends the Aktuell text family into larger-scale typographic applications.
summary: A high-contrast display serif by Thomas Thiemich that applies traditional broad-nib modulation to the Aktuell family's existing text design.
description: Aktuell Display takes the contrast and structure of Thomas Thiemich's Aktuell text design and pushes it into display territory through pronounced broad-nib modulation. The family runs across five weights from Blond to Bold, each with a matching italic, giving ten styles in total. OpenType features include small capitals, oldstyle figures, lining figures, discretionary ligatures and stylistic alternates.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Aktuell Display as Thomas Thiemich's latest display serif, diving into broad-nib contrast. The original Aktuell text design is described as a story of smaller and bigger connections, originally developed as a commissioned text design before Aktuell Display extended it into the display space.

name: Aktuell
classification_notes: Based on a traditional broad-nib contrast with roots in DDR-era type design, particularly Timeless (a DDR variant of Times New Roman), placing it firmly in the transitional serif category.
summary: A broad-nib transitional serif by Thomas Thiemich, originally commissioned for the Hochschule für Grafik und Buchkunst Leipzig and rooted in the DDR type tradition.
description: Commissioned in 2015 by Leipzig design agency Lamm & Kirch for HGB's corporate identity, Aktuell draws on the DDR-era Timeless and sits within a lineage that includes Akzidenz Grotesque BQ and the East German foundry VEB Typoart. Its short ascenders and descenders produce compact text columns with an even colour, making it well suited to space-conscious screen environments. Five weights with matching italics are supported by a thorough OpenType feature set including oldstyle figures, fractions, case-sensitive forms and extensive ligatures.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Aktuell as Thomas Thiemich's first commercially released serif, built on traditional broad-nib contrast. Originally commissioned for HGB Leipzig, its design connects to the DDR heritage of VEB Typoart and the Timeless typeface. The foundry note its strict, determined character and its particular strengths in compact text setting and screen typography, where it delivers evenness of colour and strong legibility.

name: Alto
classification_notes: Alto presents as a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a wide weight range and condensed variants, typical of workhorse grotesque superfamilies designed for versatile editorial and commercial use.
summary: A 54-style grotesque superfamily spanning three widths and nine weights, built for demanding editorial and identity work.
description: Alto is a grotesque superfamily comprising three sub-families: Alto, Alto Condensed and Alto ExtraCondensed, each available in nine weights from Thin to Black with matching italics. The family supports an extensive set of OpenType features including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions, stylistic alternates and three stylistic sets. The width range makes it well suited to both continuous text and tightly set display typography.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Alto as a superfamily of 54 styles, organised across three widths and nine weights with full italic coverage, offering broad OpenType feature support for professional typographic work.

name: Alto Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of the Alto humanist sans-serif family, sharing its true italics and down-to-earth design philosophy rather than the mechanical character typical of grotesque monospaced faces.
summary: A monospaced extension of Thomas Thiemich's Alto humanist sans family, offered in Normal and Bold weights with a condensed variant.
description: Part of the broader Alto superfamily, which spans Normal, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Monospaced widths, this cut brings true italics and humanist proportions to monospaced work. The design avoids decorative quirks in favour of practical legibility, following the approach of contemporaries such as FF Meta and FF Thesis. Latin-based language coverage is extensive, running from Western European to East European scripts.
foundry_blurb: Alto is a no-nonsense sanserif superfamily by Thomas Thiemich, first released commercially in 2008. The family began as a semester planner for the Hochschule für Grafik und Buchkunst in Leipzig and is built around four widths: Normal, Condensed, Extra Condensed and Monospaced. Alto Mono includes true italics rather than sloped romans and supports a wide range of Latin-based languages across Western, Central and Eastern Europe.

name: Cobertura
classification_notes: The family's six numbered subfamilies suggest optical or stylistic variations within a serif framework, with sample text referencing punchcutting history indicating a historically informed design. The weight range and italic pairing are consistent with a transitional serif tradition.
summary: A superfamily of 84 styles across six numbered subfamilies, each running seven weights with italics, built around a serif design with clear historical references.
description: Six distinct subfamilies — numbered 01 through 06 — each offer seven weights from Thin to Bold with matching italics, totalling 84 styles. The family supports an extensive OpenType feature set including oldstyle figures, tabular figures, discretionary ligatures, stylistic sets and case-sensitive forms. Sample text drawn from historical accounts of punchcutting signals a design sensibility rooted in pre-digital type history.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Cobertura as a super family comprising 84 styles across six subfamilies, each containing 14 styles spanning Thin through Bold with corresponding italics and a full suite of OpenType features.

name: Fakt
classification_notes: Fakt is a clean, neutral grotesque sans-serif with a broad weight range and multiple width variants, typical of contemporary European grotesque families designed for versatile text and display use.
summary: A large grotesque superfamily spanning 60 styles across three widths and ten weights, built for demanding text and display work.
description: Fakt is a grotesque sans available in Standard, SemiCondensed and Condensed widths, each running from Air to Black with matching italics across 20 styles per subfamily. The family carries an extensive OpenType feature set including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions and seventeen stylistic sets, giving designers considerable typographic control. Its neutral character and thorough width range make it well suited to complex publishing, branding and interface projects.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Fakt as a superfamily of 60 styles, spanning three width variants — Fakt, Fakt SemiCondensed and Fakt Condensed — each with ten weights from Air to Black and corresponding italics, accompanied by a broad range of OpenType features.

name: Fakt Ελληνικά
classification_notes: Fakt is a grotesque sans-serif family; this Greek extension brings the same neutral, functional character to the Greek script across a wide range of weights and widths.
summary: A 60-style Greek superfamily extending Fakt across three widths, from standard to condensed, with ten weights and matching italics throughout.
description: Fakt Ελληνικά brings the Fakt grotesque system into Greek, covering three width variants — standard, semi-condensed and condensed — each running ten weights from Air to Black with corresponding italics. The result is 60 styles sharing a consistent grotesque voice across both Latin and Greek scripts. An extensive OpenType feature set includes small capitals, old-style figures, stylistic alternates and seventeen stylistic sets.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Fakt Ελληνικά as a Greek superfamily of 60 styles, comprising Fakt GRK, Fakt SmCon GRK and Fakt Con GRK, each available in ten weights from Air to Black with italics, and offering a broad OpenType feature set including multiple stylistic sets, small capitals and alternate figure styles.

name: Fakt Cyrillic
classification_notes: A neutral grotesque sans-serif with an extensive Cyrillic character set, offering three width variants and a broad weight range typical of contemporary grotesque superfamilies.
summary: A 60-style Cyrillic grotesque superfamily spanning three widths and ten weights with matching italics, designed for Latin and Cyrillic typesetting.
description: The family runs across three subfamilies — Fakt CYR, Fakt SmCon CYR and Fakt Con CYR — each offering ten weights from Air to Black with italic counterparts, giving 60 styles in total. OpenType features are extensive, including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions, stylistic alternates and seventeen stylistic sets. The Cyrillic coverage makes it suited to publishing, editorial and identity work across Russian and other Cyrillic-script languages alongside Latin.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Fakt Cyrillic as a 60-style superfamily with full Cyrillic support, available in standard, semi-condensed and condensed widths, each spanning ten weights from Air to Black with italics throughout.

name: Fakt Stencil
classification_notes: A stencil variant of a geometric grotesque, with interrupted strokes characteristic of stencil lettering. The underlying structure is a clean sans-serif, but the stencil interruptions push it firmly into the display category.
summary: A stencil adaptation of a geometric grotesque spanning ten weights from Air to Black, with an unusually fine lower end of the weight range.
description: Ten weights running from the extremely light Air and Hair through to Black make this one of the more thorough stencil families available. The underlying letterforms follow a clean geometric grotesque structure, with stencil bridges applied consistently across the weight range. An extensive set of OpenType features includes oldstyle figures, stylistic alternates and 19 stylistic sets, suggesting careful attention to typographic detail beyond the display context.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY offer Fakt Stencil in 10 styles, from Air to Black, with a full suite of OpenType features including discretionary ligatures, oldstyle figures, stylistic alternates and multiple stylistic sets.

name: Fakt Slab
classification_notes: Fakt Slab is a slab serif companion to a geometric sans, with unbracketed serifs and a structured, even stroke weight characteristic of geometric slab designs.
summary: A 60-style slab serif superfamily spanning three widths and ten weights, designed as a structured companion to the Fakt sans family.
description: Sixty styles spread across standard, SemiCondensed and Condensed widths, each running from Air to Black with matching italics. The family carries an extensive set of OpenType features including small caps, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, stylistic alternates and a historical long-s, making it well suited to complex editorial and publication work. The breadth of the weight range, from the extreme lightness of Air and Hair through to Black, gives it considerable versatility across both text and display applications.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Fakt Slab as a super family of 60 styles, comprising three sub-families: Fakt Slab, Fakt Slab SemiCondensed and Fakt Slab Condensed, each available in ten weights from Air to Black with italics throughout.

name: Fakt Slab Stencil
classification_notes: A slab serif with stencil cuts applied throughout, giving it an industrial, utilitarian character. The bracketed slab serifs and upright construction place it firmly in the slab category, with the stencil treatment as a defining formal feature.
summary: A ten-weight stencil slab ranging from Air to Black, with an extensive set of stylistic alternates for key letterforms.
description: Spanning ten weights from Air to Black, the family applies stencil breaks consistently across a slab serif skeleton. An unusually rich OpenType feature set includes multiple stylistic alternate sets for A, V, W and other characters, discretionary ligatures for two-letter capital pairs, oldstyle figures, and historical forms. The result sits between utilitarian stencil work and considered editorial typography.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY offer Fakt Slab Stencil in 10 styles from Air through to Black, with a broad range of OpenType features including stylistic alternates, discretionary ligatures, lining and oldstyle figures, tabular figures, case-sensitive forms, historical forms and a slashed zero.

name: Fakt Soft
classification_notes: Fakt Soft is a geometric sans-serif with softened terminals and rounded details, placing it firmly in the geometric sans tradition with humanist inflections introduced through its soft stroke endings.
summary: A softened geometric sans spanning 48 styles across three widths, each offering eight weights with italics and an extensive set of OpenType alternates.
description: Fakt Soft takes the neutral, structured character of a geometric grotesque and rounds off its terminals for a warmer, less severe tone. The super family runs across standard, SemiCondensed and Condensed widths, each with eight weights from Thin to Black and matching italics. OpenType features are generous, covering small capitals, oldstyle and tabular figures, multiple stylistic alternates per letter, and a historical long-s.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Fakt Soft as a 48-style super family divided into three width variants — Fakt Soft, Fakt Soft SemiCondensed and Fakt Soft Condensed — each offering eight weights from Thin to Black with accompanying italics and a broad range of OpenType features including figure sets, small capitals and numerous per-character stylistic alternates.

name: Fakt Mono
classification_notes: A monospaced variant of the Fakt sans-serif superfamily, retaining its grotesque character while applying fixed-width proportions throughout. Classified as mono due to its equal-width spacing as its defining structural feature.
summary: A nine-weight monospaced extension of the Fakt grotesque superfamily, designed by Thomas Thiemich and released in 2019.
description: Derived from the Fakt sans-serif superfamily, this monospaced companion applies fixed-width proportions to the same clean grotesque forms across nine weights from Air to Bold, with matching italics throughout. The weight range leans heavily into the lighter end, omitting Black on legibility grounds but including the near-invisible Air. OpenType features cover multiple figure sets, fractions, superiors and inferiors, case-sensitive punctuation, stylistic alternates for a wide range of characters, and comprehensive Latin Extended coverage across Western, Central and Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Fakt Mono as a highly functional sans-serif monospaced companion to Fakt, designed to bring well-crafted monospaced proportions to an already powerful superfamily. Released in May 2019 to coincide with the launch of Type By, it is aimed at coders, UI designers and graphic designers who want a lightly flavoured but well-built monospaced font. The family ships with an extended range of thin weights, a rich OpenType feature set and broad Latin script language support.

name: Felka Slab
classification_notes: Felka Slab draws on 19th-century extreme display types such as Caslon Italian, with capitals built from stacked horizontal bars and a lively lowercase reduced to minimal strokes. Its slab construction and extreme proportions place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight display slab by Thomas Thiemich that reduces letterforms to stacked horizontal bars, referencing 19th-century extremes such as Caslon Italian while maintaining a lively, fluid lowercase.
description: Capitals are built from three fat horizontal bars without the customary thin middle bar, intensifying the visual impact common to this lineage of display types. The lowercase resists the mathematical rigidity often seen in similarly reduced designs, staying organic and rhythmic where others go geometric. The family extends across four subfamilies — Slab, Brick, Line and Leaf — with a Latin-Arabic companion for Slab, and thorough OpenType features including lining and oldstyle figures, stylistic alternates and case-sensitive forms.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Felka Slab as part of Thomas Thiemich's experimental work, rooted in 19th-century display extremes and the early 20th-century search for minimalism. The name reads as 'Lucky' in Polish. The full Felka family spans four variants — Slab, Brick, Line and Leaf — each with complete Latin glyph coverage, and Felka Slab also features a Latin-Arabic counterpart. They position it as a mature display face suited to bold, unique typographic moments.

name: Felka Brick
classification_notes: Felka Brick belongs to a tradition of 19th-century reversed-contrast display types such as Caslon Italian, characterised by thick horizontals and thin or absent verticals, taken here to an extreme minimum with capitals built from three fat horizontal bars and a lowercase reduced to two.
summary: A single-style reversed-contrast display face by Thomas Thiemich that strips capitals to three stacked horizontal bars and the lowercase to two, producing an unusually lively extreme reduction.
description: Part of the broader Felka family alongside Slab, Line and Leaf, Brick draws on 19th-century reversed-contrast types such as Caslon Italian but pushes the principle further by eliminating the thin middle bar from capitals entirely. The result is a design that reads as simultaneously archaic and minimal, with a lowercase that avoids the cold geometric rigidity common to avant-garde experiments of similar ambition. Despite its single weight, the family ships with lining and oldstyle figures, case-sensitive punctuation, stylistic alternates and broad Latin language coverage.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Felka Brick as part of designer Thomas Thiemich's experiments that grew into a mature suite of display typefaces. They frame it as '19th century urge for the extremes meeting the early 20th century search for minimalism', noting that the capitals are built on three fat horizontal bars without the usual thin middle bar, while the lowercase is reduced to just two bars yet remains lively and fluent rather than coldly geometric. Felka is available in OTF CFF, TTF, WOFF and WOFF2 formats.

name: Felka Line
classification_notes: Felka Line is a display typeface rooted in 19th-century extreme designs such as Caslon Italian, with capitals built from three stacked fat bars and a lowercase reduced to two bars, making it firmly a display face with an experimental geometric character.
summary: A single-weight display face by Thomas Thiemich that reduces letterforms to stacked horizontal bars, drawing on 19th-century extremes like Caslon Italian while maintaining a lively, fluent lowercase.
description: Part of the broader Felka family alongside Slab, Brick and Leaf, Felka Line strips capitals down to three fat stacked bars and the lowercase to just two, eschewing the thin central strokes typical of this display genre for a stronger visual impact. Where early 20th-century designers reached for pure mathematical shapes under similar constraints, Thiemich's lowercase remains organic and rhythmic. The family carries a full Latin glyph set with lining and oldstyle figures, case-sensitive punctuation, OpenType alternates and broad language coverage across Latin scripts.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Felka Line as one of four members of the Felka suite — alongside Slab, Brick and Leaf — framing the overall design as '19th century urge for the extremes meeting the early 20th century search for minimalism', brought together into a contemporary typeface by Thomas Thiemich. They note that Felka's name means 'Lucky' in Polish, and position it primarily for display typography and design projects calling for unique, bold shapes.

name: Felka Leaf
classification_notes: Felka Leaf belongs to a tradition of 19th-century reversed-contrast display faces such as Caslon Italian, where thick and thin strokes are inverted. The 'Leaf' variant is one of four subfamilies (Slab, Brick, Line, Leaf) each offering a distinct surface treatment on the same extreme structural concept.
summary: A single-weight display face from Thomas Thiemich that takes the reversed-contrast tradition of Caslon Italian and strips its capitals to three stacked horizontal bars, with a lively two-bar lowercase.
description: Part of the broader Felka suite alongside Slab, Brick and Line, this variant shares the family's core structural idea: capitals built from three fat bars with no thin middle stroke, producing a denser visual impact than conventional reversed-contrast faces. The lowercase counters the geometric reductivism typical of the genre with fluid, organic forms. Suited to display settings where bold, unconventional shapes are needed rather than sustained text use.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Felka Leaf as the product of Thomas Thiemich's experiments with 19th-century extremes, citing Caslon Italian as a precedent whilst noting that Felka's capitals omit the usual thin middle bar in favour of three stacked fat strokes. They frame the family as '19th century urge for the extremes meeting the early 20th century search for minimalism', and position it as a mature display suite now spanning four subfamilies, all equipped with full Latin glyph sets, standard and case-sensitive punctuation, lining and old-style figures, currency, mathematical symbols and accents.

name: Felka Slab Arabic
classification_notes: A radical display face drawing on 19th-century extremes such as Caslon Italian, with capitals built from three fat bars and a minimal two-bar lowercase. The slab construction and extreme weight contrast place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A single-weight Arabic companion to Felka Slab, pairing Thomas Thiemich's extreme 19th-century-inspired display construction with a purpose-drawn Arabic script.
description: Built on the logic of Felka's Latin — capitals reduced to three stacked fat bars, lowercase to just two — this Arabic companion extends the family's radical minimalism into Arabic script. The design sits within a broader Felka suite that includes Brick and Leaf subfamilies, all sharing the same underlying tension between 19th-century display excess and early 20th-century geometric restraint. OpenType features include lining and oldstyle figures, stylistic alternates, case-sensitive forms and dedicated figure-switching options for Latin numerals within Arabic text.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Felka — meaning 'Lucky' in Polish — as Thomas Thiemich's experiment that became a mature display family. They frame it as '19th century urge for the extremes meeting the early 20th century search for minimalism', with the Arabic counterparts reflecting a sense of the global village. The full Felka suite spans Brick, Slab and Leaf, each with comprehensive Latin and Arabic glyph sets.

name: Gustella Solid
classification_notes: Gustella Solid belongs firmly in the display category as an extreme reverse-contrast design drawing on nineteenth-century slab serif 'WANTED' poster lettering and the high-contrast lettering of 1970s counterculture graphics. Its exaggerated stroke contrast, inverted from conventional tradition, marks it as a specialist display face rather than a usable text type.
summary: An extreme reverse-contrast display typeface in four weights, part of Thomas Thiemich's Gustella superfamily released by TYPE BY in 2019.
description: Drawing on nineteenth-century poster slab serifs and the high-contrast lettering of hippy-culture graphics, Gustella Solid pushes reverse-contrast to a contemporary extreme with digital precision. The four weights from Thin to Bold share identical metrics with the Stripes, Inline and Boxed variants of the superfamily, allowing the separate cuts to be layered or overprinted for compound effects. An extensive OpenType feature set covers stylistic alternates, multiple figure styles, fractions, case-sensitive punctuation and full Latin coverage across Western, Central and Eastern European languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a rare cherry on the pie, designed by Thomas Thiemich and published in May 2019. They trace its lineage through nineteenth-century WANTED poster slab serifs and 1970s counterculture lettering, citing the David Bowie Hunky Dory sleeve as a touchstone, and frame Thiemich's contribution as bringing contemporary novelty and precision to the reverse-contrast niche. The superfamily comprises four families, Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed, which share identical metrics for independent use or overprinting.

name: Gustella Stripes
classification_notes: Gustella Stripes is a reverse-contrast display typeface with extreme stroke contrast and a decorative striped treatment within the letterforms, placing it firmly in the display category with roots in nineteenth-century slab and hippy-era lettering extremes.
summary: A striped reverse-contrast display face by Thomas Thiemich, part of the four-variant Gustella superfamily, designed for overprinting and extreme decorative use.
description: One of four variants in the Gustella superfamily, Stripes applies horizontal striping to an already extreme reverse-contrast structure, pushing the high-contrast tradition of nineteenth-century Western letterforms and Hippy-era graphics into a precise digital form. All four Gustella variants — Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed — share identical metrics, allowing them to be layered or overprinted for composite effects. The character set is thorough, covering Western, Central and East European Latin languages alongside stylistic alternates, multiple figure sets, fractions and case-sensitive punctuation.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a rare cherry on the pie, conceived to catch the graphic designer's eye. Designed by Thomas Thiemich and released in May 2019, the superfamily traces a lineage from nineteenth-century extreme slab serifs through Hippy-era lettering to David Bowie album sleeves, arriving at a contemporary reverse-contrast design that uses digital precision to push contrast and decorative detailing further than its predecessors. All four families share identical metrics for independent use or spectacular overprinting.

name: Gustella Inline
classification_notes: Gustella Inline belongs to the reverse-contrast display tradition, with extreme stroke contrast derived from nineteenth-century slab and Western wood type, refined here with digital precision and an inline treatment.
summary: A reverse-contrast inline display face by Thomas Thiemich, part of the wider Gustella superfamily that draws on nineteenth-century Western wood type and Hippy-era lettering extremes.
description: One of four variants in the Gustella superfamily alongside Solid, Stripes and Boxed, the Inline cut applies an engraved channel to letters already defined by extreme stroke contrast. All four subfamilies share identical metrics, making overprinting a practical option for layered effects. Four weights from Thin to Bold come with extensive OpenType features including stylistic alternates, fractions, case-sensitive punctuation and full coverage of Latin-based Western, Central and East European languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a contemporary reverse-contrast design that answers the challenge of extreme high-contrast display lettering to its fullest. Rooted in nineteenth-century Western 'WANTED' poster typography and filtered through the high-contrast lettering of Hippy culture and seventies graphic design, Thomas Thiemich's Gustella brings precision and novelty to the niche. The superfamily comprises four families, Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed, all sharing identical metrics for independent use or overprinting.

name: Gustella Boxed
classification_notes: Gustella Boxed is a reverse-contrast display typeface with extreme stroke weight inversion, descending from the Western 'Wanted'-poster slab tradition and 1970s psychedelic lettering; its dramatic contrast and decorative boxing place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A reverse-contrast display face with extreme stroke inversion, designed as part of a four-variant superfamily conceived for layering and high-impact typographic work.
description: Part of the Gustella superfamily by Thomas Thiemich, Boxed is one of four stylistic variants — alongside Solid, Stripes and Inline — all sharing identical metrics so they can be set independently or overprinted. The design draws on a lineage running from nineteenth-century Western poster types through 1970s psychedelic lettering, pushing reverse contrast to a contemporary extreme with digital precision. Ten stylistic sets, comprehensive figure variants, fractions and case-sensitive punctuation extend its utility well beyond headline use.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a rare cherry on the pie, designed to catch the graphic designer's eye. They trace its roots to extreme slab serifs of Hollywood Westerns and Hippy-culture lettering, noting how Thiemich brings novelty to the reverse-contrast niche rather than simply redrawing historical models. The superfamily — Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed — shares identical metrics for independent use or overprinting, and supports a full range of Western, Central and East European Latin languages.

name: Gustella Solid Arabic
classification_notes: Gustella Solid Arabic is a reverse-contrast display typeface with extreme stroke contrast, rooted in the tradition of 19th-century slab display lettering and 1970s hippie-influenced high-contrast typography, extended to cover both Latin and Arabic scripts.
summary: The Arabic extension of Thomas Thiemich's reverse-contrast Gustella Solid, adding Arabic script support across four weights alongside a comprehensive Latin character set.
description: Originally released as part of the Gustella superfamily in May 2019, the Arabic version followed in December 2020, bringing both Latin and Arabic script coverage to Thiemich's extreme reverse-contrast design. The four weights run from Thin to Bold, sharing identical metrics with the other Gustella variants — Stripes, Inline and Boxed — making overprinting possible across the full superfamily. OpenType features include stylistic alternates for specific Latin letterforms, mirrored glyphs, Arabic-specific ligatures, and a Latin figures option for Arabic contexts.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a rare cherry on the pie, conceived to catch the graphic designer's eye with brio. They trace its lineage from 19th-century WANTED poster lettering through hippie-era high-contrast type to David Bowie's Hunky Dory sleeve, positioning Gustella as a contemporary design that takes full advantage of digital precision to push reverse contrast to a spectacular extreme. The Arabic edition, published December 2020, expands the family's script coverage and maintains full metric compatibility across all four Gustella variants.

name: Gustella Stripes Arabic
classification_notes: Gustella Stripes Arabic is a display typeface built on reverse-contrast, ultra-high-contrast letter forms with a striped decorative treatment applied across both Latin and Arabic scripts. It belongs to no conventional classification and is best described as an ornamental display type.
summary: The striped variant of Thomas Thiemich's Gustella superfamily, combining extreme reverse-contrast letterforms with a horizontal stripe treatment across both Latin and Arabic scripts.
description: Part of the Gustella superfamily first released in 2019, the Arabic extension arrived in December 2020 to cover both Latin and Arabic scripts with identical metrics across all four weights. The stripe treatment applies a decorative horizontal fill to the already extreme reverse-contrast structure, tracing a lineage from nineteenth-century wood type through hippy-era lettering and on to precision-driven digital design. All four Gustella variants (Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed) share metrics, allowing them to be layered or overprinted for compounding effect.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a rare cherry on the pie, conceived to catch and endear the graphic designer's eye. Designed by Thomas Thiemich and first published in May 2019, the Arabic version followed in December 2020. They trace its lineage from nineteenth-century Western wood type through the extreme lettering of hippy culture and into the precision possibilities of digital type. The superfamily spans four variants — Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed — sharing identical metrics so they can be used independently or overprinted to spectacular effect.

name: Gustella Inline Arabic
classification_notes: Gustella Inline Arabic is a display typeface with extreme reverse contrast, drawing on nineteenth-century Western slab serif lettering traditions and seventies graphic design. The inline treatment and high-contrast letterforms place it firmly in the decorative display category.
summary: An extreme reverse-contrast inline typeface supporting both Latin and Arabic scripts, the Arabic extension of Thomas Thiemich's Gustella superfamily released in December 2020.
description: Part of the Gustella superfamily alongside Solid, Stripes and Boxed variants, the Inline Arabic cut applies severe reverse contrast and inline detailing to a design with roots in nineteenth-century Western wanted-poster lettering and seventies psychedelic graphics. The Arabic extension, published in December 2020, pairs the Latin characters with a matching Arabic script, making it one of the few reverse-contrast display families to span both scripts with consistent weight and metric harmony. Four weights from Thin to Bold share identical metrics across the superfamily, allowing overprinting between variants for layered effects.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella as a rare cherry on the pie, conceived to catch the graphic designer's eye with brio. They trace its lineage through Hollywood Western wanted posters and hippie-era lettering to the high-contrast display revival of the digital age, noting that Thiemich's Gustella answers that challenge to its fullest by bringing genuine novelty to the reverse-contrast niche. The Arabic extension, released in December 2020, expanded the character set to include both Latin and Arabic scripts across the superfamily's four families: Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed.

name: Gustella Boxed Arabic
classification_notes: Gustella Boxed Arabic is a reverse-contrast display typeface with extreme stroke contrast rooted in nineteenth-century Western slab and hippy-era lettering traditions, adapted here for both Latin and Arabic scripts.
summary: An Arabic extension of Thomas Thiemich's reverse-contrast Gustella Boxed, bringing extreme stroke contrast to both Arabic and Latin scripts across four weights.
description: Released in December 2020 as part of the wider Gustella superfamily, this variant extends the family's defining reverse-contrast treatment to Arabic, covering both scripts with a shared character set. The design sits within a lineage running from nineteenth-century Western 'WANTED' lettering through hippy-era typography, with Thiemich pushing the contrast to contemporary extremes made possible by digital precision. The four weights from Thin to Bold share identical metrics with the other Gustella variants, allowing overprinting across Solid, Stripes, Inline and Boxed versions.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Gustella Boxed Arabic as a rare cherry on the pie, designed by Thomas Thiemich and first published in May 2019 as part of the Gustella superfamily, with the Arabic extension released in December 2020. They trace the reverse-contrast aesthetic from Hollywood Western slab serifs through hippy lettering culture to seventies graphic design, citing the sleeve of David Bowie's Hunky Dory as a touchpoint, and frame Gustella as a contemporary revival of that extreme contrast tradition made newly precise through digital tools.

name: Pirol
classification_notes: The family's clean, structured forms and the range of alternate characters suggest a geometric sans-serif construction, with rational proportions across seven weights and seven subfamilies.
summary: A 49-style super family spanning seven subfamilies, each offering seven weights from Thin to Bold with an extensive set of OpenType alternates.
description: Pirol is structured as a super family of seven numbered subfamilies, each sharing the same weight range of Thin through Bold, giving 49 styles in total. Each subfamily offers a wide selection of OpenType alternates covering individual letterforms, figure styles, fractions and case-sensitive forms, allowing considerable typographic flexibility within a consistent framework. The numbered subfamily structure suggests systematic variation in the underlying forms rather than width or optical size differences.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY presents Pirol as a super family of 49 styles across seven subfamilies, each available in seven weights from Thin to Bold, with extensive OpenType feature support including discretionary ligatures, figure variants, fractions and a large set of glyph alternates.

name: Rosalinde
classification_notes: Rosalinde is derived from Redis pen lettering, producing a monolinear, semi-formal script quality that sits outside conventional serif or sans-serif classification. Its origins in late 19th-century handwriting tools place it firmly in the script category, though it functions as a controlled typographic family rather than a purely calligraphic face.
summary: A monolinear script family by Thomas Thiemich drawn from the Redis pen tradition of late 19th-century jobbing lettering, released as TYPE BY's inaugural typeface in 2019.
description: Rooted in the circular-point Redis nib that made monolinear handwriting accessible to commercial letterers in the Belle Époque era, the design captures a playful, semi-formal quality while remaining a disciplined typographic tool. Seven weights run from Thin to Black in a single roman width, supported by an unusually rich set of OpenType features including swash capitals, multiple alternate forms, several figure sets, fractions and comprehensive diacritics for Western, Central and Eastern European Latin languages. It sits well in packaging, editorial headings and any context where warmth and historical character are wanted without the weight of formal calligraphy.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Rosalinde as Thomas Thiemich's departure from his typically strict, neutral sanserif work, citing his soft spot for humanistic and monolinear lettershapes. They trace its origins to the Redis nib — a bent, circular-point steel pen popular with late 19th-century advertising letterers — and frame the design as capturing the light-hearted spirit of the Belle Époque while distilling it into a controlled, typographically rigorous family.

name: Remo
