classification_notes: The specimen and weight range suggest a contemporary grotesque sans-serif with a broad, workhorse character; the extended weight scale from Thin to Ultra and the presence of a 'Plus' subfamily indicate a large general-purpose grotesque family.
summary: A 40-style grotesque super family from TYPE BY, spanning ten weights from Thin to Ultra across two subfamilies with full italic coverage and an extensive set of OpenType features.
description: The family is split into Remo and Remo Plus, each running 20 styles across ten weights from Thin to Ultra with matching italics. OpenType support is thorough, covering small capitals, oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions, historical forms and twelve stylistic sets, making it well equipped for demanding editorial and typographic work. The weight range and feature depth position it as a capable workhorse across both text and display applications.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Remo as a super family of 40 styles, comprising the Remo and Remo Plus subfamilies each with 20 styles, ranging from Thin to Ultra with italics throughout and a rich array of OpenType features including multiple stylistic sets and figure variants.

name: Remo Stencil
classification_notes: A single-weight stencil typeface with a sans-serif skeleton, classified as display due to its heavy stencil construction and expressive, movement-driven glyph design rather than text utility.
summary: A mid-twentieth-century-inspired stencil sans from Thomas Thiemich, where the breaks are treated as active elements of glyph structure rather than mere interruptions.
description: Part of TYPE BY's Stencil Fonts Series, Remo Stencil sits between the late nineteenth-century Greco Stencil and the contemporary Orly Stencil, drawing on mid-century graphic design sensibility. Its strokes advance, turn and deflect with a mock-serious precision, and the heavy weight makes each stencil break read as structurally dynamic rather than incidental. A contextual alternates feature repositions breaks in adjacent repeating letters, introducing deliberate variety across a text.
foundry_blurb: Thiemich describes Remo Stencil's glyphs as constantly moving, with strokes that advance, turn and deflect in moves of mock-serious precision. The breaks articulate those moves and are amplified by the heavy weight, becoming dynamically integral to the letterforms. Contextual alternates reposition breaks in repeating adjacent letters to ensure mischievous variety. The typeface was launched in April 2012 alongside an exhibition on the history of the stencil letter held at Catapult Gallery, Antwerp.

name: Subtil Grotesk
classification_notes: A grotesque sans-serif with 16 styles across eight weights, each with a matching italic, typical of the European grotesque tradition with humanist-leaning alternates available via OpenType features.
summary: A clean grotesque sans in eight weights with matching italics and a thorough set of OpenType features including multiple figure styles and stylistic alternates.
description: Sixteen styles span Thin through ExtraBold, each paired with an italic, giving a well-rounded workhorse range for editorial and text use. OpenType features include lining, oldstyle, tabular and proportional figures, fractions, superscripts, subscripts and a selection of character alternates for a, g, l, y, Q and R. Latin Extended coverage handles most Western and Central European languages.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Subtil Grotesk as a 16-style grotesque family with eight weights and matching italics, offering extensive OpenType functionality including multiple numeral styles, case-sensitive forms, a slashed zero, stylistic alternates and punctuation at ascender height.

name: Edward
classification_notes: The page presents a broad-ranging serif superfamily with humanist proportions and a wide weight range; the sample texts referencing punchcutting history and the formal weight structure suggest a text-oriented transitional serif design.
summary: A 36-style serif superfamily split across two sub-families, Edward and Edward Plus, spanning nine weights from Thin to UltraBlack with matching italics.
description: The family is offered in two sub-families — Edward and Edward Plus — each covering 18 styles across a weight range that runs from Thin through to UltraBlack. An extensive set of OpenType features is included across all styles, among them small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions, stylistic alternates and 18 stylistic sets. The breadth of the weight range and the richness of the feature set make it a versatile option for editorial, branding and extended text work.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY presents Edward as a superfamily of 36 styles, divided into Edward and Edward Plus, each comprising 18 styles with a full range of weights from Thin to UltraBlack and comprehensive OpenType feature support including multiple stylistic sets and figure styles.

name: Lirico
classification_notes: The specimen text referencing punchcutters and 16th-century type history, combined with the name Lirico and the presence of a 'Press' subfamily, suggests a text-oriented serif with historical influences. The range of weights and italic cuts is consistent with a transitional or humanist serif design.
summary: A 24-style serif superfamily split across two subfamilies — Lirico and Lirico Press — each spanning six weights with italics.
description: Lirico comes in two subfamilies: the standard Lirico and Lirico Press, each offering six weights from ExtraLight to Black with matching italics, totalling 24 styles. Both subfamilies share a broad set of OpenType features including small capitals, oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions and stylistic sets. The Press subfamily appears tuned for demanding text conditions, while the standard Lirico offers additional stylistic alternates and sets suited to display or editorial work.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Lirico as a superfamily of 24 styles, comprising Lirico and Lirico Press, each running from ExtraLight to Black with italics. The family is built around editorial and text typography, with extensive OpenType support across both subfamilies.

name: Eva
classification_notes: Eva sits between semi-serifs like Optima and humanist sans-serifs like Gill Sans, with modest stroke contrast and varied terminal treatments that place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A warm humanist sans by Merel Wagner and Fred Smeijers, inspired by the ceramics of Eva Zeisel, with modest stroke contrast and varied terminal treatments that give it an informal, approachable character.
description: Designed initially as a single weight in 1999 and commercially released in 2006, Eva occupies a considered position between semi-serifs like Optima and humanist sans-serifs like Gill Sans or Scala Sans. Its stroke contrast is present but restrained, and the variety in terminal treatments across its letterforms creates the distinctive warmth the designers drew from Zeisel's industrially produced ceramics. Six weights with matching italics, full small capitals, oldstyle figures, fractions and superior and inferior characters make it equally suited to continuous text and personalised print.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Eva as an informal, warm sanserif that defies the popular image of a sans, with stroke contrast and freely drawn forms that give it a friendly personality at larger sizes while retaining clarity for continuous text. Named after Hungarian ceramicist Eva Zeisel, whose playful, humane approach to industrially produced forms inspired Merel Wagner's original 1999 design, the family was co-developed with Fred Smeijers and released commercially in 2006 across six weights with roman and italic designs and Latin Extended character sets.

name: Meran
classification_notes: The family presents clean upright and italic forms across a broad weight range with condensed variants, consistent with a contemporary grotesque sans-serif. No obvious humanist or geometric traits are evident from the specimen.
summary: A 36-style grotesque superfamily from TYPE BY spanning three widths, six weights and matching italics throughout.
description: Meran is a grotesque sans offered in three width variants: the standard cut, a Condensed and a SmCondensed, each running from Thin to Black with corresponding italics. All styles share a consistent OpenType feature set including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions and slashed zero. The breadth of the family makes it practical for multi-format publishing where consistent typographic voice is needed across body text and display sizes.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Meran as a superfamily of 36 styles, comprising three width subfamilies, each available in six weights with italics and a full suite of OpenType features including small capitals, multiple figure sets, fractions and case-sensitive forms.

name: Stan
classification_notes: The weight naming conventions (Blond, Normal) and the overall presentation suggest a humanist sans-serif with a refined, text-oriented character. No serif features are apparent from the specimen.
summary: A 28-style superfamily split across two subfamilies, Stan and Stan Plus, each spanning seven weights with matching italics.
description: Stan is a humanist sans-serif superfamily comprising two subfamilies, Stan and Stan Plus, each offering seven weights from Thin to Black with italic companions, totalling 28 styles. Both subfamilies share the same weight range and OpenType feature set, including small capitals, oldstyle figures, tabular figures, fractions and stylistic alternates. The distinction between Stan and Stan Plus suggests differing spacing or proportion treatments suited to complementary typographic roles.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY presents Stan as a super family of 28 styles, divided between Stan and Stan Plus, each available in 14 styles running from Thin through to Black with italics throughout.

name: Standing Type
classification_notes: Standing Type is classified as display owing to its stencil-effect construction derived from humanist minuscule calligraphy; the near-break strokes give it a decorative character that limits its use to titling and short-form settings.
summary: A stencil-effect display face by Maurice Göldner, drawn from humanist minuscule pen strokes and first shown at a 2012 Antwerp exhibition on the history of the stencil letter.
description: The design takes its rhythm from broad-edged pen calligraphy, with deliberate near-breaks in the strokes that create a stencil appearance without true stencil construction. It draws a direct lineage to typefaces such as Hans Bohn's Allegro (1936) and was released as part of the OurType Stencil Series. Available in a single Normal style with discretionary ligatures and oldstyle figures, it covers an extended Latin character set across a wide range of Western European languages.
foundry_blurb: Standing Type is Maurice Göldner's third design, based on the strokes and lifts of traditional humanist minuscules written with a broad-edged pen. The near-breaks in the letterforms create a modestly decorative, stencil-like effect without being technically stencil in execution, drawing comparison to Hans Bohn's Allegro (1936). Its first release coincided with the opening of 'Between Writing & Type: the Stencil Letter' at Antwerp's Catapult gallery on 19 April 2012, an exhibition curated by Eric Kindel and Fred Smeijers on the history and evolution of stencil type.

name: Klub
classification_notes: The weight naming conventions (Blond, Normal, Medium, SemiBold, Bold, Black) and the systematic numbered subfamily structure suggest a rational, geometric sans-serif design built for versatility across display and text applications.
summary: A super family of 66 styles spread across 11 numbered subfamilies, each sharing six weights from Blond to Black with a rich set of OpenType alternates.
description: Spanning 11 distinct subfamilies numbered 01 through 11, each offering six weights from Blond to Black, the family totals 66 styles. OpenType features are plentiful, including oldstyle and tabular figures, fractions, case-sensitive forms and a range of stylistic alternates for characters such as a, u, Q, R and G. The systematic structure makes it well suited to branding or editorial systems that require consistent variation across a single typographic voice.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY present Klub as a super family of 66 styles, organised into 11 subfamilies each running from Blond through to Black. The family includes extensive OpenType functionality covering figure sets, alternates for multiple characters, ligatures and more.

name: Couteau
classification_notes: Couteau features sharp angular serifs and brisk curves derived from knife- and chisel-cut lettering, placing it firmly in the display category. Its stencil-influenced construction and slanted roman frame make it unsuitable for text use.
summary: A single-weight display serif by Pierre Pané-Farré inspired by knife- and chisel-cut letterforms, released as part of TYPE BY's Stencil Fonts Series in 2012.
description: Drawing on the tradition of cut lettering, Couteau pairs long sweeping curves with sharp angular serifs in a slanted roman frame that gives the face a dynamic, forward-leaning quality. It shares its lineage with Berthold Wolpe's Albertus, itself rooted in chisel-cut bronze lettering, though Couteau pursues a more assertive and direct character suited to display settings. Released in a single Normal weight alongside Orly Stencil, it was developed to accompany an exhibition on stencil letters curated by Eric Kindel and Fred Smeijers in Antwerp.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Couteau as shaped by cutting, noting that knives and chisels in skilled hands produce letters that are dynamic, spirited and direct. They cite Berthold Wolpe's Albertus as an illustration of the tradition Couteau sits within, and position the typeface as friendly, self-assured and assertive across all kinds of display typography.

name: Greco
classification_notes: A stencil typeface with heavy condensed uppercase forms derived from late nineteenth-century wood type traditions, adapted with diagonal-running breaks evoking early low-resolution bitmap fonts. Classified as display due to its decorative stencil construction and bold poster heritage.
summary: A stencil display typeface by Fred Smeijers and Pierre Pané-Farré, drawing on late nineteenth-century heavy condensed wood type alphabets and reinterpreted through the visual language of early low-resolution bitmap fonts.
description: Designed by Fred Smeijers and Pierre Pané-Farré, Greco Stencil belongs to the Stencil Fonts Series released in 2012 to accompany an exhibition on the history of the stencil letter. Its heavy condensed forms trace back to wood type traditions of the 1880s, where mostly uniform glyph widths simplified spacing calculations for posters and signs. The diagonal-running breaks in its stencil cuts introduce a pattern that unexpectedly echoes early mobile phone display fonts, a conceptual leap across time that shaped the design's final character.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Greco Stencil as the notionally oldest of three sans serifs in the Stencil Fonts Series, its late nineteenth-century flavour descended from heavy condensed wood type alphabets used on posters and signs. The design was inspired by the startling visual parallel between those historical letterforms and early low-resolution bitmap fonts used in mobile phone displays, with diagonal stencil breaks adding a novel and insistent pattern to the simple underlying shapes. The series was launched on 19 April 2012 to coincide with the exhibition 'Between Writing & Type: the Stencil Letter' at Catapult Gallery, Antwerp, curated by Eric Kindel and Fred Smeijers.

name: Orly
classification_notes: Orly is a stencil typeface derived from the craft of hand-cutting paper or card stencils with simple tools, producing a geometric sans structure broken by stencil gaps. The stencil construction and display-oriented character place it firmly in the display category with a stencil subcategory.
summary: A single-weight stencil face by Pierre Pané-Farré, grown from the discipline of cutting shapes by hand with scissors and knives.
description: Pierre Pané-Farré's first published typeface, Orly Stencil was originally released in 2012 as part of the OurType Stencil Series, timed to coincide with the exhibition 'Between Writing & Type: the Stencil Letter' in Antwerp. Its letterforms are built from a spare vocabulary of shapes, deliberately shedding complex curves and fine details in the way that hand-cut stencils demand. The result is a tight, energetic sans structure that carries the physical logic of its making into every glyph.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Orly Stencil as rooted in the practice of cutting small stencils from paper, card or film using simple tools such as scissors or knives. By working within those practical constraints and avoiding complex curves, designer Pierre Pané-Farré created what they call energetic word-images from a frugal diet of shapes. A non-stencil sanserif version of Orly is noted as being in development.

name: Quadraat Headliner
classification_notes: Quadraat combines Renaissance elegance with contemporary construction, placing it in the humanist serif tradition; the Headliner variant is optimised for display sizes with two weights and matching italics.
summary: A display-sized cut of Fred Smeijers' Quadraat, offering two weights with italics drawn from the same Renaissance-influenced construction as the original 1992 text face.
description: Quadraat Headliner is a display companion to Smeijers' Quadraat Serif, available in Light and Bold with matching italics. It shares the understated noticeability of the broader Quadraat family, where vigorous outlines coexist with restrained, considered forms. The character set is currently basic, with an expanded version including multiple figure sets, ligatures and full European Latin coverage planned for release.
foundry_blurb: TYPE BY describe Quadraat Headliner as part of the wider Quadraat family, Fred Smeijers' first commercially released typeface (FF, 1992), which combines Renaissance elegance with contemporary ideas on construction. The Headliner consists of two weights of roman and italic in a single normal width, with a basic character set; an updated version with comprehensive OpenType features and expanded language support is in development.
